retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary fluoride supplements may be tablets, lozenges, or liquids such as fluoride-vitamin preparations. [19] Tablets and lozenges contain 1.0, 0.5, or 0.25 mg fluoride concentrations, and sodium fluoride is usually the active ingredient. [19] For infants, liquid fluoride supplements are used with a dropper. For children and adults, tablets are prescribed by dentists and PCPs, with instructions to chew or suck for 1 to 2 minutes before they are swallowed. This is to maximize the topical effect of fluoride. [20] One study showed an 80% reduction of caries in children who dissolved fluoride in the mouth by sucking or chewing than those who swallowed the fluoride tablets. Fluoride supplements have been prescribed to compensate for fluoride-deficient water intake. Therefore, careful consideration must be given to the percentage of fluoride content in drinking water in the patient's region and any additional sources of fluoride the patient is receiving, such as toothpaste and mouth rinse. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Community water fluoridation has been used for over half a century, and results show that the greatest effect of fluoridated water on tooth protection is in deciduous and mixed dentition. [13] [14] Water fluoridation is a simple, cost-effective method to prevent caries, and it is less than the cost of 1 dental restoration per person in their lifetime. [15] The United States Public Health Service recommends a fluoride concentration of 0.7 mg/L of water to prevent caries while reducing the risk of dental fluorosis. [16] Recently, there has been a rise in the consumption of bottled water that usually contains an insufficient concentration of fluoride. [17] In that case, alternative sources of fluoride supplementation should be considered. Also, over the decades, the protective benefits of water fluoridation have declined as other forms of fluoride delivery spread. For example, fluoride supplements are prescribed for children whose primary source of drinking water is deficient in fluoride. [13] [18] It is highly recommended by the United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) that primary care providers (PCPs) prescribe fluoride supplements starting at 6 months of age to infants whose primary water source is lacking in fluoride. [18] Fluoride supplements include the systemic ingestion of fluoride in the form of dietary tablets, lozenges, or drops.

retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride gel is principally indicated in patients older than six years old. Under this age, the risk of experiencing adverse effects when accidentally swallowing the gel, particularly nausea and vomiting, outweighs the potential benefits of using this agent. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients recover within 24 hours [18] [1] [4] . In one study, approximately 50% of reported cases required hospital admission [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Swallowing Study -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician provides food and drinks of different consistencies to chew and swallow under fluoroscopy. The selection of test food is based on the swallowing difficulty being investigated. The speech and language therapist carefully selects the varying viscosities used to account for the potential severity of dysphagia posed by the subject, as well as any known anatomic or functional abnormalities. These test foods/liquids are pre-mixed with barium as the contrasting agent, though water-soluble contrast agents such as gastrograffin can also be utilized. With the radio-opaque bolus, images are obtained at different stages of the swallowing process to highlight and analyze the cause of dysphagia (see Image. Stages of the Swallowing Process). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Gluconate -- Mechanism of Action -- Hydrofluoric Acid Burns
retrieved_docs.content: In treating hydrofluoric acid burns, calcium is a mainstay of treatment that binds to fluoride ions, effectively neutralizing them to prevent further toxicity. Furthermore, fluoride can cause hypocalcemia, possibly due to the formation of fluorapatite (Ca(PO)F) salt, thus decreasing free calcium levels within the serum. Calcium gluconate directly replenishes ionized calcium levels within the blood in cases of fluoride-induced hypocalcemia. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride mouthwash helps prevent dental caries by increasing fluoride levels in dental plaque and saliva. [19] The mouthwash must be used at a different time of the day than when toothbrushing to increase its availability in the biofilm and optimize its topical effect. [28] The concentration depends on the frequency of use. Over-the-counter solutions of 230 ppm sodium fluoride are indicated for daily rinsing, and those containing 900 ppm for weekly use. [29] They are not recommended for children under 6 years due to the risk of swallowing, as the risk/benefit ratio favors risk. [6] Parents must supervise the use of fluoride mouth rinses in permanent dentition, as this has proven to be more efficacious than unsupervised utilization. Fluoride Varnish, Gels, and Foams

The mechanism of action attributed to calcium carbonate involves participation in electrolyte activity, modulation of calcium ion balance, and contribution to acid‑base homeostasis. Clinically it functions as an antacid for gastro‑esophageal reflux disease (GERD), reflux esophagitis, dyspepsia and diarrhea, providing symptomatic relief by neutralizing gastric acidity. Calcium carbonate is also noted as a downstream component of natural mineralized structures such as pearls. Sodium monofluorophosphate, the active fluoridated compound in the dental pastes, is present at concentrations from 0.22 µg/100 µg to 2.43 mg/g and is formulated together with calcium carbonate (0.0076–0.0088 mg/mg) to prevent dental caries. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: The calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, sodium fluoride combination is linked to the compound known in Czech as fluorid sodný, indicating that this multi‑ingredient formulation is derived from or mapped from fluorid sodný. Fluorid sodný itself is identified as having the specific form sodium fluoride F‑18, a radioactive isotope of sodium fluoride. Consequently, the combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride can be understood as a preparation that incorporates the properties of sodium fluoride F‑18, reflecting its origin from fluorid sodný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: The combination product containing calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is conceptually derived from the sodium‑fluoride entity (fluorid sodný). Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the sodium‑fluoride group, are described as having a fluoride (fluoridy) relationship, and are identified as a type of “other mineral supplements in ATC,” the same ATC category that includes selenium supplement. Within the sodium‑fluoride classification, a specific form exists known as sodium fluoride F‑18. Together, these relationships show how various mineral‑based formulations—ranging from multi‑component calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride to single‑ingredient fluoride and selenium supplements—are organized under the broader ATC grouping of other mineral supplements, highlighting their shared pharmacological context and classification.

The Standardized MedDRA Query “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي (SMQ)” aggregates several gastrointestinal functional disorders—reflux gastritis (الْتِهابُ المَريءِ الارتجاعي), diarrhea (جَيّار), dyspepsia (عُسْرُ الهَضْم), and gastro‑esophageal reflux disease (مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ)—to facilitate identification and management of unspecified digestive dysfunctions. Collectively, magnesium chloride, calcium carbonate, and sodium monofluorophosphate are integral to the formulation, stability, therapeutic efficacy, and safety of products spanning endocrinology (insulin delivery), cardiology (cardioplegic solutions), ophthalmology (irrigants), gastroenterology (antacids, electrolyte solutions, SMQ‑defined disorders), infectious disease (antibiotic granules), nutrition (supplements), and oral health (fluoride dentifrices). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active fluoride in numerous dental products, including Aquafresh Kids Cavity Protection (1.10 mg/g), McKesson Fluoride dentifrice (2.43 mg/g), Amerfresh (0.22 µg/100 µg), Trade Joe Anticavity Peppermint (0.76 µg/100 µg) and low‑dose toothpastes (0.0076–0.0088 mg/mg). Its anticavity efficacy highlights the importance of recognizing both active and inactive components in oral‑care formulations. Calcium carbonate also has therapeutic applications for gastrointestinal functional disorders—reflux esophagitis, diarrhea, dyspepsia and gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease—grouped under the “unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder” SMQ. Constipation is clinically associated with vomiting; meclizine hydrochloride can alleviate dizziness and may prevent vomiting, linking vestibular symptom management to emesis control.
